The curtain has risen on Australia’s entry to the 60th Venice Biennale – a vast hand-drawn chalked family tree stretching back 65,000 years before colonial settlement.

Artist Archie Moore “records” 2400 generations of ancestors – drawing on intensive research into his English, Scottish and Indigenous heritage – across walls and the ceiling of the cube-like structure of the Australian Pavilion.

Artist Archie Moore and curator Ellie Buttrose at the Australian Pavilion.Credit:Andrea Rossetti

Moore is only the second solo First Nations artist to stage a work at the biennale regarded by some as the Olympics of the international art world.

The reimagined genealogical chart sprawls 60 metres and was intensively researched for the biennale, which opens on Saturday and runs until November 24.

Australia’s installation was unveiled at a media preview this week as the Israeli artist Ruth Patir and curators of the Israeli pavilionkept their doors shut, saying they would only open their exhibition when a ceasefire and hostage release agreement had been reached.

Before then, pro-Palestinian protesters had unsuccessfully argued for the biennale to ban Israel over its conduct of the war in the Gaza Strip.

Commissioned by Creative Australia, Moore’s origins story makes a strong contrast to Australia’s previous entry to the world’s oldest biennale by Melbourne musician and noise-making artistMarco Fusinato,who performed live every day of its opening with stacked amps, discordant guitar riffs, and a large LED screen.

Among the chalked names in the Australian work is Moore’s great-uncle, who accidentally killed his own father in a dispute over unpaid wages and spent nine months in Brisbane’s Boggo Road Jail.

The name of his great-grandmother Jane Clevin also appears. Samples of her hair and drawings were taken from her family by the anthropologist Norman Tindale when he visited Boggabilla in 1938.

From that information Tindale mapped out a family tree in a Western anthropological fashion, Moore said. “It shows the comparison of the linear, Western idea of lineage, to the vast and complex relatedness of an Aboriginal kinship system.”

Moore has left gaps – literal black holes – to represent massacres, the introduction of deadly diseases, and the destruction of cultural and linguistic knowledge.

The full scale of Moore’s kith and kin inside the Australian Pavilion at the Venice Biennale.Credit:Andrea Rossetti/The Commercial

The structure of his sprawling family tree upends the idea of there being original ancestors, an Adam and Eve in the Christian tradition. “There is no beginning to point to–rather Archie shows the vast abyss of time from which life springs,” said curator Ellie Buttrose.

“By researching his family Archie demonstrates his connection to one of the longest-continuous cultures in the world and declares his sovereignty and the sovereignty of his people. By tracing back thousands of years he also shows how everyone on Earth has common ancestors, a reminder that we are all kin.”

Among the many kinship names recorded inkith and kin,Moore also includes pejorative names given to Aboriginal people, sometimes appearing in media reports and official government correspondence, from picaninnie, to full blood, old Mary, and half-caste.

A reflection pool has been built in the pavilion’s centre over which more than 500 document stacks have been suspended.

These contain mainly coronial inquests on the deaths of Indigenous Australians in police custody dated in Moore’s lifetime.

Moore’s genealogical tree up close.Credit:Andrea Rossetti/The Commercial

With its use of chalk and chalkboards, the work references educational materials from Moore’s childhood, Buttrose said. “He was only taught about Australian colonial history and nothing about the continent’s First Nations history. The artwork shows how First Nations history and knowledge exceeds the limits of standard educational forms.”

The chart affirmed the status of Australia’s Indigenous people as custodians of some of the longest-continuous living cultures in the world, and the sharp decline in First Nation’s languages, she said.

“Extending across the Pavilion’s black ceiling, the inscribed names resemble a celestial map; evoking the ancestors’ resting place. In the First Nations Australian understanding of time, past, present and future are co-present,” she said.

Asked what he wanted audiences to take away from the exhibition, Moore said it was to “show the strength and vastness of First Nations Australian kinship and history, which has survived in spite of colonisation and will continue into the future”.

title: First peek at artist Archie Moore’s entry for the Venice Biennale
author: Linda Morris
description: Australia’s entry to the Venice Biennale is a vast chalked family tree stretching back more than 65,000 years to the beginnings of time.
